It details a comprehensive change history from 2014 to 2025, highlighting updates such as error code additions

structure.title: Introduction and functional overview
structure.summary: Transformers in AUTOSAR systems enable data transformation mechanisms to linearize and transform data. These transformers can be concatenated into chains, which are executed by the RTE for intra-ECU and inter-ECU communication. The chains are specifically configured to handle data transformations in both communication scenarios. The RTE manages the execution of these chains to ensure proper data handling. The transformation process is integral to enabling structured data exchange within and between ECUs.

structure.title: A transformer provides well defined function signatures per each communication relation (port based and signal based), which is marked for transformation. The function signature depends on the transmitted data elements (Client/Server operation signature or Sender/Receiver interface signature) only. The output of a transformer will be always a linear byte array.

A more powerful system can chain multiple transformers where the input of the first transformer in the chain gets the data from the RTE. Each following transformer uses the output of the preceding transformer as input. All transformers following the first one then have generic signature with just a byte array as IN and OUT parameter. Such an architecture could be used to design systems, where you can flexibly add functionality like safety or security protection to a serialized stream.

nodes.title: file structure
nodes.summary: The code file structure for transformers is defined by [2, SWS BSW General], as all transformers are BSW modules. This standard applies uniformly to all transformer implementations. Deviations from the general structure are explicitly specified in the SWS documents tailored to individual transformers. The reference [2] provides the foundational framework for the file organization. Specific transformer variations are documented in their respective SWS specifications.

nodes.title: Header file structure
nodes.summary: The header file structure of transformers is defined by [2, SWS BSW General], as all transformers are classified as BSW modules. Deviations from this general structure are explicitly specified in the SWS documents tailored to individual transformers. The reference to [2, SWS BSW General] establishes the foundational framework for header files across all transformer implementations. Specific transformer SWS documents address any exceptions or modifications to the standard structure. This approach ensures consistency while allowing for necessary customization in specialized cases.

structure.title: Requirements Tracing
structure.summary: The "Requirements Tracing" section establishes traceability between SRS (Software Requirements Specification) and SWS (Software Specification) requirements, detailing which SWS requirements fulfill specific SRS requirements. For example, [SRS_BSW_00407] is satisfied by [SWS_Xfrm_00057], [SWS_Xfrm_00058], and [SWS_Xfrm_00059], while [SRS_Xfrm_00002] is addressed by multiple SWS entries. The table structure links each SRS requirement to one or more SWS requirements, ensuring alignment between high-level specifications and implementation details. This tracing confirms that all listed SRS requirements are covered by corresponding SWS requirements, with some SRS items mapped to multiple SWS entries

structure.title: A transformers takes data from the RTE, works on them and returns the output back to the RTE. It can both serialize/linearize data (transform them from a structured into a linear form) and transform (modify or extend linear data) them (e.g add a checksum).
structure.summary: Transformers are BSW modules in the Communication Service Cluster that provide communication services to the RTE

structure.title: A transformer is no library because transformers can hold an internal state but they can work as well stateless.
structure.summary: A transformer is not a library because it can maintain internal state, though it may operate statelessly. Stateful transformers cannot be used as libraries, while stateless ones can. Transformers are connected into chains, with the RTE executing them in the specified order from the [3, System Template], which defines the maximum chain length (255 transformers) and the sending-side order. The receiving-side order is

structure.title: A further example of data transformation is shown in Figure 7.2. Here the use-case of intra-ECU data transformation is addressed.
A transformer gets its input data via a pointer which destination can vary in length. Therefore, an implementation of a transformer has to cope with input data which are longer than expected.
structure.summary: A transformer

structure.title: A transformer will usually work on the data and/or generate some protocol information which are stored in a header and/or footer of the output. Therefore it needs a place to write the result to. Transformers can work with two buffer handling modes: In-place buffer and out-of-place buffer. Which one is used is determined by the configuration in the [3, System Template] and influences the transformer’s interface.

nodes.title: Transformer Classes
nodes.summary: Transformers are categorized into classes due to their diverse functionalities. Different types of transformers exist, each fulfilling entirely distinct purposes. This classification reflects the varying roles and operational characteristics of transformers. The categorization ensures clarity in identifying and applying the appropriate transformer for specific applications. The text emphasizes that these classes

structure.title: A transformer class shall contain all transformers which provide similar functionality. At most one transformer of each transformer class shall be allowed per transformer chain.
structure.summary: A transformer class groups transformers with similar functionality, and each chain may include at most one transformer from each class. The current transformer classes defined are Serializer, Safety, Security, and Custom. Additional transformer classes may be introduced in future AUTOSAR releases. This structure ensures functional consistency and avoids redundancy within transformer chains. The restriction to one transformer per class per chain maintains clarity and prevents conflicting transformations.

structure.title: A serializer transformer accepts complex data (either a Sender/Receiver data element or a Client/Server operation with its arguments) or no data (Trigger communication) from the RTE and provides the resulting byte array as an <u>ISignal</u> or part of <u>IPdu</u>, which is finally transmitted to the receiver by the COM stack.
structure.summary: The serializer transformer processes complex data (Sender/Receiver elements, Client/Server operations with arguments) or no data (Trigger communication) from the RTE, converting it into a byte array as an ISignal or part of an IPdu for transmission via the COM stack. It must serialize all possible complex data inputs using a defined algorithm, supporting only "new-world" variable-size arrays (excluding "old-world" types per constraints [constr_1387], [TPS_SWCT_01642–01645]). The deserializer transformer (on the receiver side) must return all or a subset of deserialized data to the RTE, as mandated by SRS_Xfrm_0

structure.title: A safety transformer protects the communication against unintentional modifications to ensure a safe data transmission.
structure.summary: The safety transformer is mandated to protect inter-ECU communication for safety-related SWCs, ensuring data transmission safety. It must guarantee the correct order and content of data transmissions. These requirements are fulfilled through mechanisms like sequence counters and checksums, which meet safety standards. The transformer safeguards against unintentional modifications, maintaining data integrity. Examples include implementing sequence counters and checksums to enforce correct transmission order and content.

structure.title: A security transformer protects the communication against intentional modifications to ensure security of the bus communication.
structure.summary: A security transformer is required to protect inter-ECU communication for security-related SWCs, ensuring data authenticity, integrity, and freshness. These requirements (SRS_Xfrm_00001) mandate protection against intentional modifications to secure bus communication. The implementation example includes adding sequence counters and checksums to fulfill these security criteria. The transformer’s role is critical to maintaining secure and reliable data transmissions across the bus. All specified security properties are explicitly addressed through these mechanisms.

nodes.title: Custom
nodes.summary: Custom transformers are not standardized by AUTOSAR but can be defined by any party in the development workflow to address non-standardized transformation needs. They are implemented as CDDs (Component Description Documents) to specify their behavior and requirements. This approach allows flexibility for custom logic without relying on AUTOSAR's predefined transformer specifications. The use of CDDs ensures compatibility with existing AUTOSAR tools and processes. Custom transformers thus provide a mechanism for extending the framework beyond its standard capabilities.

nodes.title: Error Handling
nodes.summary: Transformers return errors to the RTE as return codes, which determines whether to continue or abort the transformer

structure.title: AUTOSAR CP R25-11
structure.summary: The section highlights that soft errors in early transformer stages of a chain may be masked by subsequent hard errors in later transformers. For example, the E2E transformer may trigger a soft error upon detecting a corrupted message, but the SomelpXf could override this with a hard error if deserialization fails, masking the E2E state machine's transition. The E2E state remains INVALID until valid messages are deserialized, causing the application to observe the

structure.title: **[SWS\_Xfrm\_00031] Errors of serializer transformers** Upstream requirements: SRS\_Xfrm\_00010
structure.summary: The section outlines error codes for serializer transformers, categorized into **Soft** and **Hard** errors. **Soft errors** include `E_NO_DATA` (0x01) for missing data and `E_SER_PAYLOAD_LENGTH_EXCEEDED` (0x40) for payload length mismatches. **Hard errors** cover critical failures such as `E_SER_GENERIC_ERROR` (0x81), version mismatches (`E_SER_WRONG_PROOL_VERSION` 0x87, `E_SER_WRONG_INTERFACE_VERSION` 0

nodes.title: Errors of Security Transformers
nodes.summary: The "Errors of Security Transformers" section outlines error codes and their classifications for security-related issues in the E2E Library. Values 0x04, 0x24, 0x34, and 0x44 are reserved for internal use. Errors are categorized as soft or hard, with soft errors indicating recoverable issues (e.g., invalid communication, repeated counters, or disabled state machines) and hard errors signifying critical failures (e.g., uncheckable runtime errors or safety violations). Specific errors like E_SAFETY_NOSM_ERR and E_E2E_HARD_SAFETY_ERR disable the E2ESStateMachine

nodes.title: Development Errors
nodes.summary: The section defines development errors in the Xfrm module under [SWS_Xfrm_00061], referencing upstream requirement SRS_BSW_00337.

nodes.title: Runtime Errors
nodes.summary: The "Runtime Errors" section explicitly states that there are no runtime errors. This indicates the system or code under discussion does not encounter exceptions, crashes, or other execution-time issues. The absence of runtime errors suggests stability and correctness in the software's operation during execution. The documentation provides a definitive confirmation rather than discussing potential errors or mitigation strategies. This statement is concise and does not imply any conditional or hypothetical scenarios.

nodes.title: Production Errors
nodes.summary: The "Production Errors" section states there are no production errors. Error codes are categorized into **E_OK** (0x00, no error), **Soft** errors (0x01–0x7F), and **Hard** errors (0x80–0xFF), which are transformer-specific. Additional error codes include **<MIP>_E_UNINIT** (0x01) for uninitialized modules, **<MIP>_E_INIT_FAILED** (0x02) for invalid configurations, **<MIP>_E_PARAM** (0x03) for incorrect parameters, and **<MIP>_E_PARAM_POINTER** (0x04) for invalid pointers. These codes correspond to specific error conditions in API service calls.

nodes.title: Extended Production Errors
nodes.summary: The section titled "Extended Production Errors" lists and specifies the errors associated with transformers in extended production contexts. It serves as a chapter dedicated to detailing these specific error categories. The content focuses on enumerating and defining the technical issues relevant to transformer manufacturing or operation under extended production scenarios. No additional details beyond this are provided in the text.

nodes.title: XFRM_E_MALFORMED_MESSAGE
nodes.summary: The section defines the Extended Production Error **XFRM_E_MALFORMED_MESSAGE**, which must exist for every transformer where the parameter _XFRM_E_MALFORMED_MESSAGE is set. This requirement is tied to upstream specifications **SRS_BSW_00466** and **SRS_BSW_00469**, ensuring the error is explicitly defined for all relevant transformers. The error is triggered when a malformed message is detected during transformer processing. The text emphasizes the mandatory existence of this error code for compliance with the specified requirements. No additional details about error handling or conditions are provided.

nodes.title: Error Notification
nodes.summary: The error **XFRM_E_MALFORMED_MESSAGE** is defined in [2, SWS BSW General] and occurs when a transformer fails to produce output due to malformed input data. The error indicates that the input's format does not conform to the specific transformer's specification, leading to an inability to generate valid output. Detection criteria classify the error as **Fail** if the input is malformed and **Pass** if it conforms. Monitoring occurs on every transformer execution, with no secondary parameters or time requirements specified.

nodes.title: Imported types
nodes.summary: The "Imported types" section specifies that transformers must adhere to the upstream requirement SRS_Xfrm_00002, which mandates the use of `ImplementationDataTypes` defined by RTE in the transformer’s Module Interlink Types Header file. This header file is referenced as per SWS_Rte_07503, ensuring consistency with the RTE’s type definitions. The requirement ensures that all transformers correctly import and utilize the specified data types from the RTE’s interlink header. No additional types or details beyond this are permitted. The focus is on strict compliance with the defined types and their source file.

It lists imported types for the **Rte** module (Rte_Cs_TransactionHandleType in Rte.h) and multiple types from **Std_Types.h**, including Std_ExtractProtocolHeaderFieldsType, Std_MessageResultType, Std_MessageTypeType, Std_ReturnType, Std_TransformerForward (draft), and Std_VersionInfoType. Additionally, the {Mip}_ConfigType is defined as a structure in

nodes.title: Function definitions
nodes.summary: The "Function definitions" section outlines the naming conventions for transformer IDs in the BswModuleEntry, governed by the presence of XfrmVariableDataPrototypeInstanceRef and specific Com module configurations. TransformerId follows patterns like `Com_<ComSignalName>` or `ComponentName_p_o`, where components are derived from SwComponentPrototype, PortPrototype, and VariableDataPrototype references. The naming ensures clarity and avoids ambiguity in data transformation contexts, even though the pattern isn't strictly required for functionality. The transformer function signature depends on XfrmVariableDataPrototypeInstanceRef, potentially generating multiple functions for a single XfrmSignal if the same ISignal/ISignalGroup is referenced by multiple XfrmImplementationMappings. This structure aligns with upstream requirements (SRS_Xfrm_00002)

nodes.summary: The "_Mip_Init" section references upstream requirements SRS_BSW_00407 and SRS_BSW_00411. It defines "Mip" as the Module Implementation Prefix of the transformer, as specified in [SWS_BSW_00102]. The term "Mip" is explicitly tied to the transformer's implementation structure within the software architecture. No additional details about functionality or initialization steps are provided in this section. The summary preserves all technical definitions and citations without extrapolation.

structure.title: Configuration specification
structure.summary: This section outlines configuration parameters and their organization into containers, referencing Chapter 10.1 for foundational concepts and a parameter specification template. Section 10.2 defines the structure and parameters of transformers, which are configured at the system level using [3, System Template] and at the software component level via [5, Software Component Template]. These configurations enable the generation of a basic **EcuC** for the transformer. The chapter retains Chapter 10.1 to ensure comprehensive understanding of the specification.

nodes.title: Containers and configuration parameters
nodes.summary: The section outlines configuration parameters for a general transformer setup, referencing Chapter 7 (Functional Specification) and Chapter 8 (API specification) for detailed parameter meanings. Specific transformers utilize the EcuC container, which is populated with their specific content. The EcuC is generated automatically from DataTransformationSet information, as the transformer generator has access to all required data. The ECUC_Xfrm_00014 entry defines the EcucModuleDef Xfrm, specifying its structure and role in the configuration. This process ensures consistency and automation in transformer configuration.

nodes.title: XfrmGeneral
nodes.summary: The **XfrmGeneral** container is part of the Xfrm module's configuration, containing general parameters with a multiplicity of 1 and no post-build variant support. It includes the **XfrmDevErrorDetect** parameter, a boolean that enables (true) or disables (false) development error detection and notification, with a default and pre-compile time value of false. The module supports only the **VARIANT-PRE-COMPILE** configuration variant. Additional parameters like **XfrmInstanceld** and **XfrmVersionInfoApi** are also included under XfrmGeneral. The container's parent is Xfrm, and it is required for defining general module

structure.title: Referenced Meta Classes
structure.summary: The "Referenced Meta Classes" section describes two meta-classes: **ArrayImplPolicyEnum** and **BswModuleEntry**. **ArrayImplPolicyEnum** defines configuration options for array payload implementation, with literals like `payloadAsArray` and `payloadAsPointerTo Array`, specifying how arrays are structured. **BswModuleEntry** represents a C-function prototype in a BSW module, with attributes such as `callType`, `executionContext`, `isReentrant`, and `isSynchronous` defining service behavior and context. The class is aggregated under `ARPackage.element` and is specific to the AUTOSAR Classic Platform. Additionally, **AtomicSwComponentType** is referenced but not detailed in the text.
